frontmatter.title: Transformations of homomorphisms of natural models

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:

Let { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi , \Psi }}{ N } be two (lax) homomorphisms from M to N. A transformation { \Phi } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ \Psi } of homomorphisms from M to N is by three cells
such that commute with the components of \Phi and \Psi in the following sense:

frontmatter.title: Algebraic description of the bicategory of natural models

mainmatter.p: A natural model is described by the following structure in the bicategory of (small) categories:

ol.li: a sequence of discrete fibrations E \xrightarrow {p} U \xrightarrow {c} \mathcal {C}:
a right adjoint { \mathbf {1} } \xrightarrow {{ \diamond }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the terminal functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ * }}{ \mathbf {1} }:
a right adjoint { U } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ E } to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }:

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:
A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } of natural models is given by the following data:
The last cell { *_{N} \circ \Phi _{ \mathcal {C}} } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi _{*} }}{ *_{M} } always exists canonically and is invertible, but we include it as it plays a role in further definitions.

A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } is called strong when the 2-cells \Phi _p and \Phi _c are invertible, and the right mates \Phi _q , \Phi _{ \diamond } of \Phi _p , \Phi _{*} depicted below are invertible, ensuring that context extension and the empty contexts are preserved respectively:

It is seen by drawing string diagrams that if the underlying cells of a transformation of homomorphisms of natural models are invertible, then the transformation itself is invertible. Furthermore, it follows from the discreteness of E and U over \mathcal {C} that the transformation laws uniquely determine the components \alpha _E and \alpha _U if they exist. As such, the only data in a transformation is the lowest cell \alpha _{ \mathcal {C}} acting on contexts.

There are several possible bicategories of natural models, corresponding to different allowances of laxness. All of these are, in fact, (strict) 2-categories — but we see this as a meta-level statement as it concerns definitional equality.

By “the” bicategory of natural models, we shall refer to the one in which 1-cells are strong homomorphisms and the 2-cells are invertible transformations. This (2,1)-category is precisely the one described by Uemura; although it is certainly interesting to consider other variations, the (2,1)-category that we consider here has various important properties including presentability in the sense of Lurie. We hope that the simplified picture of natural models offered by string diagrams will facilitate the study of the entire landscape of bicategories of natural models.
A natural model is described by the following structure in the bicategory of (small) categories:

ol.li: a sequence of discrete fibrations E \xrightarrow {p} U \xrightarrow {c} \mathcal {C}:
a right adjoint { \mathbf {1} } \xrightarrow {{ \diamond }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the terminal functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ * }}{ \mathbf {1} }:
a right adjoint { U } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ E } to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }:

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:
A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } of natural models is given by the following data:
The last cell { *_{N} \circ \Phi _{ \mathcal {C}} } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi _{*} }}{ *_{M} } always exists canonically and is invertible, but we include it as it plays a role in further definitions.

A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } is called strong when the 2-cells \Phi _p and \Phi _c are invertible, and the right mates \Phi _q , \Phi _{ \diamond } of \Phi _p , \Phi _{*} depicted below are invertible, ensuring that context extension and the empty contexts are preserved respectively:

There are several possible bicategories of natural models, corresponding to different allowances of laxness. All of these are, in fact, (strict) 2-categories — but we see this as a meta-level statement as it concerns definitional equality.

By “the” bicategory of natural models, we shall refer to the one in which 1-cells are strong homomorphisms and the 2-cells are invertible transformations. This (2,1)-category is precisely the one described by Uemura; although it is certainly interesting to consider other variations, the (2,1)-category that we consider here has various important properties including presentability in the sense of Lurie. We hope that the simplified picture of natural models offered by string diagrams will facilitate the study of the entire landscape of bicategories of natural models.

frontmatter.title: Algebraic description of the bicategory of natural models

mainmatter.p: A natural model is described by the following structure in the bicategory of (small) categories:

ol.li: a sequence of discrete fibrations E \xrightarrow {p} U \xrightarrow {c} \mathcal {C}:
a right adjoint { \mathbf {1} } \xrightarrow {{ \diamond }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the terminal functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ * }}{ \mathbf {1} }:
a right adjoint { U } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ E } to { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }:

mainmatter.p: Let M and N be natural models, as depicted schematically below:
A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } of natural models is given by the following data:
The last cell { *_{N} \circ \Phi _{ \mathcal {C}} } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi _{*} }}{ *_{M} } always exists canonically and is invertible, but we include it as it plays a role in further definitions.

A lax homomorphism { M } \xrightarrow {{ \Phi }}{ N } is called strong when the 2-cells \Phi _p and \Phi _c are invertible, and the right mates \Phi _q , \Phi _{ \diamond } of \Phi _p , \Phi _{*} depicted below are invertible, ensuring that context extension and the empty contexts are preserved respectively:

It is seen by drawing string diagrams that if the underlying cells of a transformation of homomorphisms of natural models are invertible, then the transformation itself is invertible. Furthermore, it follows from the discreteness of E and U over \mathcal {C} that the transformation laws uniquely determine the components \alpha _E and \alpha _U if they exist. As such, the only data in a transformation is the lowest cell \alpha _{ \mathcal {C}} acting on contexts.

There are several possible bicategories of natural models, corresponding to different allowances of laxness. All of these are, in fact, (strict) 2-categories — but we see this as a meta-level statement as it concerns definitional equality.

By “the” bicategory of natural models, we shall refer to the one in which 1-cells are strong homomorphisms and the 2-cells are invertible transformations. This (2,1)-category is precisely the one described by Uemura; although it is certainly interesting to consider other variations, the (2,1)-category that we consider here has various important properties including presentability in the sense of Lurie. We hope that the simplified picture of natural models offered by string diagrams will facilitate the study of the entire landscape of bicategories of natural models.
